speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Lola Gamble Clyde took place at her home south of Moscow on October 12, 1976. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: And they're driving it. And a man named Huff is driving it. And Huff came from Grangeville. They took it back to the World's Fair at Chicago and won a prize. They took it down to the Pacific in Yukon. Alaska Pacific Yukon exhibition in Seattle in 1999 and won a prize. And there they are. And he's got that picture hanging there on the wall. He has a lot of other old -time pictures. And in it are many replicas of old -time things. And he really knows an awful lot of history and stuff. You just love Uncle Bill. Well, let me ask you about his social life then. I wanted to know one thing about the women in those days. I've had the impression that a lot of them were not in the best of health. That there was a lot of sickness among the women who were here in the early days. And there wasn't all over the country in the early 1900s.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you find that to have been so when you think about it?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, I think so too. It seemed to be one of the great subjects of conversation was the ill health that they all had. And part of it was that they didn't have enough outside interests, I think. And then every ache and pain was magnified. And as they used to say, she feels every pain, you know. And it was, maybe some of it was that. But in those days they had children, one right after the other, you know. Only ten children in ten years. And that's pretty hard on women's health. And then there was lots of hard work required of the early pioneer women. And I imagine their health wasn't very good. And doctors were very inadequate. They had so few of them. And in the very early days, most of the children were ushered into the world with the help of a midwife and that sort of thing. So they were, really the child bearing was hard, was really hard on them. It took its toll physically. Yes it did. It took its toll physically.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that because they didn't know enough about, at the time, about how to take care of that with the least trouble?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, I suppose so. And many babies were born without much medical attention, without any much prenatal care. And very little postnatal care. And doctors were few and far between.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well what kind of effects would that have on the mothers then, to not have doctors' care? What would that do to them? Would that just mean they wouldn't get strong? They would become more weak?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: That's right. And some of them died in childbirth if there were complications developed. They lost, mothers died in childbirth because they didn't have medical assistance at the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that fairly common out here?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, I think as a little child, I remember two of our neighbor women dying in childbirth. And that was quite a, you know. The large size families is one too that I worry about. I've noticed that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That it seems like the early families here were very large, weren't they?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, that's right. But the way my readings indicate that birth control was not unknown during that period of time, so that it could have been practiced perhaps if families had wanted to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Or didn't they have any knowledge of birth control techniques?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, I don't know. A lot of them had the feeling that lots of children was lots of security. And a lot of them, their religion kept them from practicing what they did know. And I just wouldn't be able to answer, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when you talk about the security in the children, was that the idea that, and I'm just trying to figure this out from all that people have said, was that the idea that a big family, you'd have a lot of people to help on the farm? Was that the thinking behind it?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, it could have been, although I've heard some of them say, one father can take care of twelve sons, but twelve sons can't take care of one father. And I think that was quite true too. Each of them grew up and went on their own, you know, went on their own way, merry ways. And I don't know that there was so much taking care of the old folks, went on as could have, but I think religion entered in to a lot of their thinking, that they didn't have, not necessarily because they were Catholics, but many of the religions taught that the Lord giveth and the Lord taketh away, and this is just the will of the Lord that I'm giving these children.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you mean the Protestant denominations?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, the Protestants too. Many of the Protestant people felt that way, that these were gifts straight from heaven, and they accepted them as that, you know. Okay, but then what about, you know, we talked some before about women being chattel and that kind of thing, and really being in almost servitude because of all they had to do for the husband and children.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now wouldn't it stand to reason that the only way that they could be free of that would be if they didn't have big families? I mean, to have a big family was to accept that idea, right?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, that's right. Well, I just don't know what the feeling behind all that was with the early day women, but lots of them prided themselves about, I remember one old lady saying she did, 16 children and she lived to raise eight of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did that mean? She lost the other eight?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, she lost the other eight.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In childbirth or when they were babies?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, as small ones, there were different epidemics. Moscow went through the Black Bithyria scourge here a couple of times, and at one time, some of the early histories report that half the children in the town of Moscow died with Black Bithyria.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, really?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: I didn't know that. It was that bad. It was that bad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Gosh, about when do you think that would have been?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Oh, it would be in the very early, in the early 70s, 1877 maybe, in 78. Along through there they had a bad, a bad epidemic of Black Bithyria. Well, to talk then about, thinking about this, what women accepted for their lives, it sounds to me like,

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: well, what I'm wondering, do you think that the women then questioned whether they should have an alternative to just being homemakers and raising families? Did that only come later that that was questioned?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well... I mean, you know what I'm... Yes, I know what you mean.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that the only way they saw to live at the time?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, I can only answer from my own mother's viewpoint. My mother was an educated woman, and she had taught, she taught in the first girls' school in Canada, in the Angela College in Victoria, B .C., and when she moved to this pioneer settlement, she wanted my father to let her go out and teach school. And, oh, that was against the principles of the old country. The man made the living. It was his privilege and his right, and he wouldn't let her go. But I've often heard her say that she wished that my father had let her go and teach school at the same time because they could have used the money so well. And nearly all the farm women had cows, and they would milk them and sell the cream, and they got most of the egg money, and that was considered their money that they could do as they pleased. But the grain, the crop, had to go in for paying for land or machinery and go for debts. But the egg and the cream money, the women could have that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But they spent it not on themselves, did they?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: No, they spent it on the children and on food. Most of it went on the children and on food for the table. So, in other words, she was really exclusively in control of the children and the food for the table. That's right. That was her calling.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the household furnishings and, you know, the furniture and all that? Was that all hers, too?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, and once again, I can hardly answer it. The men did most of this buying, as I remember it, the Scandinavian men especially. But some of them took great pride in their houses and buying a piano for their daughters. That was the epitome of gracious living, was to be able to get a piano and give the girls music lessons. When I was a little child. And there were some of the families that could do that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, say something like sofa and chairs and that sort of thing, would that usually be the husband or wife who would make the buying?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, the major purchases. Most of the major purchases, like that much money, would be made by the men. The men would decide when they would get it and some of them took pride in having well -furnished houses and that sort of thing, you know. Well, I guess that, I know this kind of thing is, these sorts of things are pretty imponderable, they're difficult to figure. But I'm asking you some of these things because of the reading that I've done in now, when they're looking at women's history and social history in the 19th century. These are the kinds of questions that they're asking.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why was it this way and how was it?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: So they're trying to generalize on what, you know, some of these things that women's life.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But for other women, like besides your mother, was your mother unusual in her interest in getting out of the home to teach or do you think that it was common, most women would have if they could have?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, yes, I think if they had been fitted, but there's so few were educated in those days, you know, there were so few trained, I think most of them would have tried. Because I remember in later years when they opened the pea picking factory, when Moscow became the pea capital of the world and women could go to town and get jobs in the winter picking out the rogue peas from among the other peas. Oh, lots of the women went out and got jobs and would drive to town every day to pick peas. And that gave them a feeling of independence. They got money for doing it. And many of the married women whose children were then in school would even get a job in town to bring in a little extra money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They come from the rural areas? They come from Troy and furthers?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, oh yes, they came from Troy and come into Moscow to pick peas.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When do you think that started, in the 30s or before?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Maybe after 1925 people started picking peas because Moscow then had established a good market for green peas.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what about the beginnings of women teachers in the schools? Now, in the early days I had heard that it was largely men, is that true? Largely men teachers in the rural schools?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, there were some, yes, but right around here that was a very well accepted position for the women was to grow, the girls. They would grow up to be teachers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But I mean now, I'm trying to figure when, in the early pioneering, in the 80s, were there women teachers back that early in large numbers?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: No, I think that most of the early teachers were men teachers. So your mother would probably have been unusual in having been a teacher at that time. Yes, that's right, because they weren't trained for it. No, that's right, most of them were men teachers. And it wasn't until maybe in the 90s that we saw women coming in, but that was a highly respected job for the young girls. They would grow up to be teachers or they could be nurses. That was a highly respected job.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they getting, do you think that it started then because that's when the girls were able to go to Lewiston Normal and the university?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, I think so, yes, because in 1895 came the Lewiston Normal and that was a fine opportunity for the young girls to go on and get education.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And they were starting, they were going there right away to become teachers?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, as soon as Lewiston Normal started. We had no school of education at the University of Idaho when it first opened.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, there wasn't?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: No, it was just the liberal arts. But the girls did go there, but not with the idea of becoming teachers so much. But after Lewiston Normal started, that was to train teachers and they did go down there. And that was an ambition with many of them was to go on to school, those that liked school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well then, do you think, would it be fair to say that probably the large numbers of, or the influx of women into rural school teaching started at when Lewiston Normal started? Or do you think it would have started before?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: No, I think it started with Lewiston Normal very much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Before that they wouldn't have really had a place to get it?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: No. And for many years the student nurses had to go to Spokane to the hospitals there to get nurses training. They'd go up to Sacred Heart and get training there. But with the coming of Lewiston Normal, that certainly started turning out our own rural teachers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they going to Cheney before Lewiston Normal for teaching?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Or do you know how that, because I've heard of women who were your contemporaries did go to Cheney too. Yes, many of them went to Cheney. And many of them from right around here went to Cheney. Yes, Cheney was established, Cheney Normal was established ahead of Lewiston Normal. Somewhat earlier. Yes, somewhat earlier. But you know, then it seems to me that the problem in a way was pushed back further because now once they were teaching in the rural school, then a young woman could teach until she married.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Right?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: I mean that was built into the way that things worked. Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now how did people feel about that? I mean you've told me some of how, that you didn't mind stopping teaching, but do you think that at the time that after this freedom of being a teacher, making your own money and making your own way, that most of the women didn't mind to just give that all up?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well I think a lot of them didn't give it up. A lot of them went on teaching after they were married until the Depression hit. And then married women couldn't get jobs because they felt that the single women needed the jobs or a married man who was head of a family should have the jobs. So married women weren't allowed to teach in this immediate vicinity during the Depression. No one would hire them. But before that, people, teachers had told me in the rural county that they, not only was it custom that they stopped teaching when they married, but in a lot of school districts they had it in the contract that you couldn't be married. That's right, yes. That's true. That's true. That doesn't seem like giving much choice.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, that didn't give them much choice, did it?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: They had to be really in love with the men they married, you know, to give up their independence.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean an encouraged romantic marriage?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: That's right, an encouraged, you know. Yes, that's right. No, but in terms of, really, just from the point of view of fairness, it seems that that meant that for most of these people they had a choice between career and marriage. That's right. That's about the way it has been, you know, through the years. I'm just wondering, for instance, why they had that as a rule in the rural schools, and so many of them, that you couldn't be married and teach. I mean, to give a person the option would seem a lot fairer than to say you can't teach if you're married. Well, many of the rural schools did hire married women up until the time of the Depression. Many did. Yes, many of them did. I knew quite a few married women who taught around Moscow in the immediate vicinity, and they went on teaching and that sort of thing. But after the Depression hit, then most of the schools wouldn't hire a married woman because they felt that they should save the job for some single woman or for some head of a family.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did most of these women that did quit teaching to raise their family and then come back to teaching, I mean, were they older women?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, I think so. I think most of them were older women or women without children or women with just one child. They weren't women with a lot of children and a lot of household responsibilities. I think that was pretty well established.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How old would the youngest child have to be before a woman would feel comfortable with going back to teaching?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, I think they had to be a school age, and then they could take the child with them to school or put them in school someplace. I think that was the way most of them did, as I remember.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, I don't know. It does seem to me, for instance, with yourself, the choice that you made to marry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you find it somewhat difficult to make the adjustment of being totally at home instead of out in the sun?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: I had a very wonderful understanding, lenient husband, very indulgent husband. And he just said, Lola, you just go and do your thing and I'll do my thing. And he let me sit on school boards. At one time, I was never a board farm wife. I just sat on all the boards in town. At one time, I was on 14 different boards. Forleta County in the town of Moscow, school boards, library boards, Red Crowds, Whitman Hospital, mental health, county health unit. There was a dull moment.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you didn't find it boring to sit on all those boards?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: No, I just had fun.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How about in your younger years when you were first married?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, when I was first married, I tell you, I was just so busy. We had a great depression hit and we went down to 28 cents, you know. And I had about 17 hard men to cook for and four little children, one right after the other. Four children all under four years of age. I didn't have time for any regrets or anything else. I was just too busy trying to keep food on the table for everybody, you know. Well, you know, let's talk a little bit about unmarried women. I'd be curious to know what you think. It seems from what I've read and some of what I've seen, people like Catherine, for instance, who didn't marry. That's right. Now, do you feel that...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How would you explain why a lot of people didn't marry in those days?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: It seems that there in some ways were a lot of that back in those days of people deciding not to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it that the women were too educated for the men who were around or something like that?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, I suppose women, right men, just hadn't come along, let's put it that way. And someone weren't satisfied with the kind of men they met, you know. And you don't always meet just the right person. And hello! ...pressure on young women to marry. Yes, I think so too. I think the mothers were always afraid the girls would be old maids, you know. And they'd urge them to get out and meet young people and that sort of thing, you know. That that was the accepted thing to do. And all the mothers thought their daughters should be the bells of the ball, you know. And follow along in the way they had done. I think that there was that feeling among them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, but talking about the choice between marriage and career, do you think that some of the women did make a choice for a job in independence and freedom? That it was not just that nobody came along, that there was something positive?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, I think that's right. I think that there were many of the highly intelligent girls who didn't marry because they did like their work and their jobs and they'd have to give up too much. I think. I think that's true. Well, it seemed to me that with the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you say that there was a generally higher level of education among the girls than the boys?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, I think so. Their services weren't needed so badly on the farm. The girls, you know, the boys were needed so badly farming in the rural communities. And they'd always figure, well, when this boy got out of school, sometimes that was just right out of the eighth grade, well, we'll buy another piece of land and start him up farming. And that went on a lot and in the very, very early times, the boys stayed home and helped their fathers in the spring and in the fall. And maybe in the very early days, it would just be a couple of months in the summer they had school. And later on, it was just a few months in the winter that the older boys were allowed to go to school. And then they couldn't be working out in the fields and so on, so they'd go to school for a little while. So the girls, because they weren't needed, had the freedom to go to school. Yes, the girls had more freedom to go to school. Yes, I think that's very true. Okay, now we look at our educated young woman.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could she easily find herself in a position where there wasn't anybody around who was her intellectual, who could be her companion that way?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, I think so. I think that's right. I think they might have educated themselves out of the market. I think sometimes that happened. I think often that happened.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that men tended to be afraid of brainy women or not like brainy women?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: That's right. I think that's true, yes. I think most of them can be aware of these school moms. They'll boss you around the rest of your life. Although in the early days, the school moms were generally quite the bells of the ball. They set the social pace, what the new teacher wore, and what she did was very much the pattern for the rest of the girls of the community.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really? Of the young wives, too?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, of the young wives, too. They'd watch what kind of hats and what kind of dresses the new school teacher was wearing. And that sort of was a pace setter in the outline.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did she find herself in that position, that she was the pace setter?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Was it because of being from... Yes, a lot of them came out from the towns to teach in the rural communities. And they had a little more money of their own. They could buy things like that. And they were expected to be well dressed and that sort of thing when they taught school. Coming from Lewiston, they might have been closer to the fashion. That's right. They came up from the big town of Lewiston and they might know what the latest styles were. What were the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The styles, were the styles changing in those days? Were they different from year to year?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: A little bit, quite a little bit. Yes, they'd wear high button shoes sometimes, and sometimes it would be Oxford's. Sometimes that sort of thing. Yes, the styles changed. I can remember my mother wearing the big leg -a -mutton sleeves on her coats. And then I remember the mother hubboards for the kitchenware, you know. And later on it was stylish to have high waists and low waists. And even the little girls in school would pattern after what their teachers had.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do people actually have a little bit of extra money clicking around that they could follow fashions to?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, sometimes, you know, sometimes if they got a little extra cream check they would buy a little something extra. Or if you were buying maybe a new dress you'd try to get the style. Well, I'd heard that everybody had a black dress, that black was the big thing. Yes, that's right. They had a black silk dress and they had that to wear to church on Sunday. That's quite common, a nice black silk dress.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, the average farm wife at that time, like your mother and the women of her generation, how many dresses would they own?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, just about one good one would be all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And that would be black?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, a nice black silk one to wear to church or to something, a funeral or to a wedding. And they were few and far between, you know, they weren't a big invitational affairs like they are now or like they were for a while. And if you had any one good, decent dress they called it for church and funerals. And Mother Hubbard's the rest of the time, largely.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Mother Hubbard's were?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Kind of loose fitting dresses. Boy, you got lots of phone calls. I'm glad, I hope they are calling. ...wear is not being very healthy, like... Yeah, and of course it's a tight...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, was that kind of a constricting and dumb idea?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, these wasp waists, you know, that was going a little too far, I'd say. Cinching them in, you know, I think that could be very uncomfortable anyway. I don't know that it really hurt any of their organs, but I think it would be very, very uncomfortable.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I think that helped liberate women when they threw away the corsets, don't you? Well, when did that happen? Was corset wearing, did tie corsets worn fairly late?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, I think so. I think they were wearing them up until the First World War anyway. Yes, they were cinching them up tight and wearing, getting them the wasp waists and the fashions of the pre -war days, of the pre -World War I days. All show little small waists, you know, and that was a sign of great elegance and beauty. And regardless of how tight they had to cinch them, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They get them?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Sure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say wasp waists, why wasp?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, you know how wasps are, they have, you know, the hornets, they have little tiny... Oh! In the middle, their middle is very small, so that's a wasp. And that's what they call... So they had to have a wasp waist, they had to pinch them in in the middle, and then they could be big above on big hips, that was all right. But right around the middle they had to have a wasp waist.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this something that affected, that all classes did? I mean, would the relatively working class families do the same thing?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, a lot of the women who really went out, you know, and worked in the field, and lots of the early women did. They would go out and shock the bundles and pitch hay right along with their men folks. I don't think they did it, no. I think it was more the pseudo -society ladies that went in for the wasp waists. And of course pregnant women, that would be pretty hard to come by, they just wore the mother hubbards and covered all the defects.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well the farm families, in those families, the average farm family, would they wear the tight waists?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Oh, I think not, no. I think most of the farm women were too busy getting their cows milked and the chickens taken care of, you know. So this is the town... It's more of a town. Yeah, more of a middle class town, ladies that had to follow along a little in the society trends. I think most of the farm women were just really pretty busy milking cows and raising gardens and getting the fruit can for winter and all that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that a pretty clear, distinct line, do you think, between the rural farm women and the city people who were really middle class?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, I think so. I think that's right. Well, to talk about the differences between them, would the town middle class ladies be much more involved in the social... Oh yes, much more, yes. They had their own rich parties and their party going and often in the early days around Moscow they would hire, they could get hired help very cheap and they would hire the nice little girls from off the farms that were growing up, 16, 17 year old girls, and they would work in these society homes of Moscow and that was part of their training. They learned how to make fancy desserts and how to cook and how to serve and...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would be the sign where we could say these people are middle class and other people are working class? And would that be the sign of the middle class family if they had a girl working in the home?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, lots of the middle class had hired girls that came in and worked and of course the... I forget what they called them, they called them the upper tans, the upper tans, you know. They would be like the McConnells, you know, and folks like that. They had serving quarters even, a little special room where the serving girls slept. And these girls helped take care of the children and helped prepare the meals and they learned many arts and many skills at cooking that their parents couldn't teach them because they didn't have the abundance of extra fancy food and different things on the farms that was available in town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But say there would be a lot of other middle class families that would also have girls helping, right?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Besides say the upper tans. Oh yes, that's right, yes. There were middle class families too that would get girls and many of the farm women too would get a young neighboring girl to come and help in the harvest field of time when they had lots of extra men. That was just standard practice that one neighbor's young girls would go over and help out the other neighbors when they were harvesting and so on, help take care of the children.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But they wouldn't be living year round in the country?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: No, they wouldn't be year round in the country, no. That was largely a city way, or at least the town of Moscow way that you went to town and stayed in the home there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So it would only be say during harvest right at the busiest time?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, the farm women had help, that's right. Yes, right at the harvest time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Maybe for a month?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yeah, a month, six weeks, something like that while they got the combine, the threshing and that sort of thing done. The binding and threshing of those days. So in other words, to really spot a middle class family at that time, you could say that they would be likely to have a girl living in and helping them and the women would wear these tight, these washed waists. That's right, these washed waists and dress up and go to little parties, little afternoon teas and parties and take part in the church work. And that was the middle class doings. In other words, they had leisure. Yes, that's right. And the country women didn't have that kind of leisure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would that be fair to say?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: I'd say that's very fair. Yeah, I think that's right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the church work that the women were doing in those days? How would you characterize it?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: I know for instance, and this was on a grander scale, but I know that the missionary work was tremendously important and many young women who were educated actually gone overseas. Oh yes, well part of the missionary work that fell to the women's missionary societies, they called them ladies' aides in those days or missionary societies and that sort of thing. And they did lots of sewing for the missionaries overseas. They'd make layouts for babies and diapers and they were continually doing some kind of work to help the missionaries that they sent to the fields of China and Japan and Africa. They were always doing something to raise money to send medical supplies or something to the missionaries that they supported. And then the ladies' aides of the churches, part of their job was to keep the churches cleaned up and bring flowers on Sunday and bake cookies and carry on the evangelism between the families of the church and visit the bereaved and the sick and help each other in any way that they could. And besides raising money to carry on the work of the church, of the local church, they'd have bazaars even as they do today, you know, and church suppers where you all brought food and then sold suppers out. They'd have baked food sales and sell food and the farmers would bring in produce from the farm and often the minister was paid in kind. He would get a hog at butchering time, he'd get half a beef, he'd get so many sacks of potatoes. That was just an old farm custom that some of the nicest things you raised you gave to the minister.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, to carry on the work of evangelism between the families that you mentioned, what did that mean?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, it meant we will love one another, we will comfort them in sorrow and sickness and distress. We would visit them, we would bring, and the main thing was bringing things, you know. You brought the glass of special jelly, you baked one of your very special cakes, you baked one of your nicest pies if there was a death in a neighboring family, and you called and you brought something in your hands as the positive proof of your sympathy. You didn't come empty handed, you brought something to show that you shared in their loss and in their sorrow and in their time of need, that you were there to help them and be a spiritual comforter as well as a physical comforter. You could get in and turn out a washing for the family if the mother was sick, you could bake up a big batch of bread, you could do various things like that. So in other words, this evangelism worked mainly in times of need for other families. That's right, yes, that's right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did it work?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Oh, I think so. Yes, I think it was, I think it made the people who did it maybe gain more than the people who received, because it's more blessed to give than to receive. But you felt very grateful for your own good health and for your own good fortune, and you wanted to share with somebody else and try to make them, try to comfort them in what they were going through. And I think it worked both ways. It was nice to know in sorrow that you were remembered by other people, and it was nice to go and you gain too. There was a spiritual growth on both sides.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For the church work, how much of, how important was it for the women as a part of their overall social activity? Was this the main social activity for most women?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, very definitely. The social life of the whole community sort of centered around the churches. The young people were taken care of. There would be parlor games for them to play, and a meeting ground for young couples so they could pick their partners for life that way. And there was an effort to keep them all within their own churches. That was a great thing. The young people of this church would get together for Halloween parties, for Christmas parties, for any holiday. There was a great getting together of the young people and the little children. There were games and plays and programs centering around the youth of the church as far back as I can remember. One of the big purposes of the church was to help bring up the coming generations and the belief and the traditions of the old folks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you think that the churches in Moscow did much more of this, say, than the churches in the small towns that had to draw on most of the country folk to come in?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, I think so. I think maybe Moscow was more able because there were more people in it to start out, but there were more homes, more people to exchange. And the country folks, it was hard to get into town. It took a team of horses to get there and a buggy. It took a couple of hours to make the trip in and out. So there weren't so many families in the horse and buggy days that could get into Moscow to take part in the church life, and often out in the little rural schools. The teachers would put on a Sunday school program Sunday morning for the children, at least, of the community. And sometimes they would be preaching at the schoolhouse Sunday afternoon. The ministers would come out and do a little evangelism right in the community because it was hard before the coming of the cars. For the families, it took about half a day to get uptown and go to church an hour and then drive back home. The whole morning was spent that way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So many families would just stay and not go to church?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, they just stayed home and didn't go. That's right, until the coming of the car.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't some of the neighborhoods have just laymen too?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Oh yes, some of their own people did it. Oh sure, many of them. Many of the more devout people who had a little training would get up and read the Bible and they'd have little discussions. And the women that could sing would sing, and nearly every school had some sort of little reed organ of some kind. And they'd put on their own little church programs on the Sunday morning.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In Moscow, most of the women who were involved in the active church were, were they mostly the middle class women who had the leisure to take part?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, that's right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many times a week for just roughly, or how often would they get together at the church for one thing or another?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: I mean it would be more than just on Sunday. Oh yes, yes, well, yes they had women's clubs that met, and women's associations that met. And then they had sewing circles and quilting bees, you know, and missionary societies that would meet. Oh I imagine there wouldn't be a week go by, but there'd be a couple of meetings at the church for the women. And there was always choir practice for them to go to, and most of the women were the singers. Lots of them would be women. There'd be a few men that sang in the choir, but it was another place for the young folks to get together to, and try to get some of the young boys and the young girls to come sing too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well were the Ladies Aid and Missionary Society one and the same organization?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: They were practically the same. Some people called them the Ladies Aid and others called them the Women's Missionary Society. I see. Nowadays we call them the Women's Associations. That means all the women of the church, and they do mostly Bible study. And then we have different sewing groups too that meet to sew for the missionaries. So that's the way nearly all the churches are constructed. Well it strikes me that in those days that from what we're saying that the church activity was definitely predominantly a women's activity, and the men were much less involved.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that true?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: I think that's true. Yes, I think the women were the ones that really supported the church and did all the extra things about the church. Many of them had altar guilds that kept the altars decorated and washed the linens and the laces that they used on some of the church altars. It was the women that saw that all of that was ready. And nowadays I see lots more men taking part in it. But in those days that was the women's prerogative. And it was the women's social outlet more. The men had other outlets that they could go to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think it would be true to say that a lot of men had to be dragged to church if they'd go at all in those days?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: I think there were a lot of them that had to be. There were some of them who had been trained to go to church and went. Especially I think among the Scandinavian people. It was so much a part of their lives in the old countries. And they were very devout and got their families to hook up the horses and go on Sunday morning. They did it. They'd been well versed in the work of the church. But among more of the Americans, of the longer people who had been Americans longer, been citizens of this country longer. They weren't so devout. But the people who had come from the Scandinavian countries, and that's a lot of them around the Moscow settlement, they were very devout in their attendance to their church.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that women by and large could really apply what they were preaching about and what they were praying about in church in their everyday lives to? Or do you think it was separate from what they did?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well I think in the very early days it was very much a part. It was their reward. They might not have much right now at the present, but in the sweet by and by they would be rewarded. All the old time songs are along that line about there's a gold mine in the sky. And they were all going to have their reward later on. If they had suffering here on earth, that was more stars in their crown. And I think with the very early settlers, a lot of the women had that feeling that it was all right to sacrifice and suffer now because they would get their reward.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think they really believed in it?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes I do. I believe that they believed that very... and when someone died it was always, well they've gone to their reward. We'll try and not grieve because they have mounted the battlements. But that changed... Well yes, in 75 years I think it's changed a lot. Well I don't think then, but you say the very early ones were very strong on that. Yes they were strong in between. Well I think gradually there was a tapering off and a questioning over the years. But I can remember as a child these women, all of them were going to get their reward in heaven and it was just all right. They'd do a little suffering here on earth, it was only temporary, all that would pass away. And they had mansions in the sky that they were building and their treasures they were laying up in heaven where the moths, where thieves could not break in nor moths corrupt. And every one of them had that feeling that that was the way it was going to be. So that was...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that was true of both working, of both the farm women and the middle class women?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: It's a hard question. It's a hard question, I just don't know, I just don't know. But I think many of the farm women felt that way, that this is just a temporary, this is just a veil of tears we're passing through and we will overcome, we will have our reward in the next world and everything's going to be all right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was their attitude towards death then? Were they very matter of fact about it in those days? Did they just take it in stride?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Death is a kind of traumatic experience today. Yes it is. And I'm wondering what it was like then. Oh they suffered terribly too then. The women suffered terribly then in the loss of their children and in the loss of the loved ones. I think that somehow all the balm and gilead doesn't seem to ever assuage it much. We should, you know, if we want to show our faith and exemplify our faith in what we preach, we should be able to stand tall and strong at the time of death and say, well let's not hold this good man from his reward, he's earned it, heavens he's mounted the battlements, let us rejoice. But I never saw many of them doing it. I've seen the later ministers do much better at it. We've had ministers in our Presbyterian church that say, we have come today to celebrate the life of the person that's dead. And I think that's good. I think it's a much healthier way. Instead of we're here to mourn and grieve over the passing, you know, I think it's better to say we have come to celebrate the life that he lived. And that makes sense to me. But it's only in the last twenty years I've heard that approach. Now today we've come to celebrate, not his death, but we have come to celebrate the life that this good man lived. Well I'm wondering about whether the commonness of death in those days, as compared to now, would have had an effect. Because people must have lived with more uncertainty, knowing that their children might not grow up, knowing that death could take you at any time. I mean nowadays death only seems to become real when people become old. But most of the population doesn't seem to think they're immortal.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They think they're going to live forever, don't they?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yeah. But I mean in those days I wonder how living with death, you all the time... Yes, and so many of the children dying and so on. Oh I think it's like Rachel of old grieving for her child and will not be comforted. I saw it go on and on, the grieving for their children. And really if they were firm believers, if they'd gone to this place of eternal glory, they should have been rejoicing. That they were out of all temptation, out of all sorrows way. But in all my years I've never seen anybody that wasn't just grieving for their child and cannot be comforted.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the death of older people? In those days, wasn't there a real tendency for the old in the families to stay with the family that they cared for?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, they stayed with the family and there was always that talk about the vacant chair, and we shall meet but we shall miss him, you know, there will be the vacant chair. And I don't know somehow, it seemed that they were always missing the one that was gone. And there was much talk about it. Nowadays I don't think we spend so much time talking about it. We know it's like the ripening of the fruit and the falling of the autumn leaves. It's going to happen, it's inevitable and we accept it better. But in those days every life seemed to be so precious. And there was much talk about, oh, when grandma was alive and grandma did this and we'll never have any more of those wonderful things she did. And that's kind of a defeatist attitude. Well I read, I have read about death in the 19th century that people, that many of the farm, rural people, they really, it was very important to them, for example, the way that their relative died. If they died alert and knowing what was going on and if, particularly if they had a religious death. Yes, oh yes. I should write in through the pearly gates.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that something that was important?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, I think so. A lot of them, especially the younger people that were alert, they all were trying to bridge that last few minutes and seeing if the person who died had looked into heaven and seen what was there. I remember a young girl who died and the mother always was so comforted because the last words the girl had spoken was, I hear the angels singing. And the mother said she had just looked, I know that my daughter looked straight into heaven. Because she said to me, turned and said to me as she died, I hear the angels singing. And that was of great comfort to the mother. So, but most of them, as I say, they were no better than Rachel of old. They grieved for their children and could not be comforted. It only took time to wear it out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it the usual when someone was dying in the family for the family to attend on the person?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Oh yes, they all sat up with the dying person and they sometimes sent for the minister and the minister would come and sit up and somebody dying quite a ways off, they'd get on the horseback and ride to sit up with the family. So and so is dying tonight. We must go and sit with the family and they'd go and sit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would a close family friend sit too?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, the close family friends would sit and sometimes the minister would come and sit all night to help the grief, the people through the grief. Yes. Well it certainly sounds different. Yes it does, it sounds different. It really does. And yet some of those old time traditions are so ingrained in you, you feel that it has to be done. When we lost our little Mary Louise Perrine last spring at the nursing home, I went over and sat up all night until she died with her. I felt, oh I just felt I had to. I couldn't leave her there to die alone and although I knew she wouldn't be, you have to do it alone anyway, you do it alone. But I thought I just had to be there and I went and sat up all night with her. So those are some of the traditions that stick with you. What about keeping, having the grandmother for instance or the uncle, staying with the family when they're really quite old.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that that made a great difference to them as individuals in their last years to be with the family?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: I'm thinking of the contrast with today when so often people do go in the homes. Yes, they all in the homes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In those days, would that person remain a functioning part of the family?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well they stayed with them because it was, it's just that family loyalty, you know. I kept my brother Bert here who grew old and you knew him. I kept him here until he was 86 and he could no longer function, he had no longer any control of his bladder or bowels or anything. And I kept him until I just couldn't do it any longer. And then the doctor ordered him to the nursing home. He said, Lola you can't do it, you've got to. And I agree you doctor, I have to do it. I felt I was neglecting my duty. That I should have seen my brother down into the grave and not let him, not put him out in the nursing home. And yet I, no one knows better than I, I helped build all three of them, how wonderful they are and what good care, far better care than I can give them. But I suppose it's just selfishness on our part. We don't want to give them up. We don't, we fool ourselves into thinking we can do everything they need and that they're better with us than if they were there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well in the old days in those families, did most or many families have an old member in the family?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Oh yes, that's the only place they could keep them, you know. Yes, and until we built the two new nursing homes in Hemmings, that's been the first nursing home I think we built about 1955. Before that there was no place to go but to the poor farm out there. Which I understand was an awful place. Oh it was a terrible place, yes it was awful. And nobody with any kind of family pride would think of allowing their relatives to go there. It was just people who had, just the derelicts that went there. And I think maybe the new nursing homes with all their lovely facilities, maybe some of that old stigma sticks with us. I know some people who don't want to go to Ladau Condolescent, well that's the county. You know, just because they call it Ladau County Condolescent Home and that's so ridiculous because everybody pays there. Everybody, even the county cases, well they're getting Medicaid, they're getting Medicare. Everybody pays at both of them. None of them are county cases.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well do you think that it had an effect on the family to have an elderly people in the home?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: I mean nowadays it seems like it's just a husband, wife and a kid and that's it. In those days it was a bigger unit. Yes, well I think it gave them a feeling of continuity. I don't think the children suffered any by it. I think the children had a feeling of, well when we get old that's the way we are. And they could accept it. Here was grandpa and grandma and they were getting a little more frail, a little more feeble. And they'd have to make allowances. Grandma couldn't run and wait on them so much, you know. And I think it gave them a great feeling of continuity and a feeling of responsibility. And we've got to grow up and be able to take our parents places. And we've got to take care of our parents the way they take care of grandpa and grandma. I think, I don't think it was bad at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that for grandma and grandpa that they could function longer because they still had something to do for the family?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, I think so. Some of them felt very helpful. They were always, I can remember grandma's, Earl's grandparents, knitting stockings for my children and making dresses. And oh, they took such pride in what the children were doing and what they did at school. They always wanted to go to the school programs and see what they were doing. And it's a great sense of continuity and it's a good picture of life, I think, for the children to grow up. And this is it. This is the process. Here are the parents and there are the grandparents and here are the uncles coming. And we'll have a family get together on Thanksgiving and then there'll be Christmas and then there'll be Easter. And all the folks will come. I think it made the family a very important unit. More so than now. More so than now, I think. I think so. You know, one thing I really want to ask you about when we're talking about the women, middle class women and country women.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now what about the status distinctions between them?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: I have a feeling that there were quite a few and that they were rather subtle. Oh yes, yes, surely, yes. This sense of superiority. Did it make the country people, or at least it seemed like it was intended for the country people to feel backwards. Oh yes, surely, surely. And, you know, as we know in any society there are always some people wanting to put somebody down, you know. And these were just the rural people and these children even in the school. Oh, these children come in from the country, you know, and they aren't as well kept. Like I say, with the coming of the electricity and water on the farms you couldn't tell the rural kids and the city kids. They were all cleaned and scrubbed, you know. But before that there was a distinction. The children wouldn't be quite as clean and there was lots of talk about B .O. and about these smelly kids, you know, and that sort of thing. Yeah, there was more of a distinction. And the mothers who had to pick the peas, their kids would come to school and their dresses wouldn't be ironed. And the days when dresses had to be ironed and starched and stiff. And there was a feeling, you know, among the other kids that, oh, these are the kids from the country. These are the kids from the other side of the tracks and that sort of thing. But that's been going on a good many years, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what did the country people say back to that? What did the country women, the farm women have to say to their city sisters who were a little uptown? I mean, did they feel that they were just putting on airs?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, I think we, I, of course I as a country wife was quite different, you know, because I couldn't have cared less. I couldn't have cared less. But, but I remember, I'll tell you this one, because this really happened. There was a lady lecturing at a historical club and I was just a little fat country wife and I was invited to come into this great historical lecture. And this woman had toured Europe with a group of university children, university students. So she was going to give a talk on conditions in Germany and all these places she'd been. So it was, Germany had already gone into Poland. There was already the murder of the six billion Jews. They'd done all that. We were on the brink of war ourselves. And this woman got up at a historical club in front of all the selected town and started in telling about why she said Hitler did just right, murdering that bunch of Jews. And it went on and she talked about, oh, about 40 minutes about these terrible Jewish people. So at the end she asked for questions and I, who was just a guest of some folks there, got up and said, well, my Christian conscience won't let me sit here and listen to you talk that way. This was a crime against humanity and Hitler has, has committed an unspeakable crime and how you can stand there and defend him, I don't know. Said I to the lady.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So the lady took one look at me and she didn't answer, but she said to the hostess, said, who is that woman anyway?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: I don't think I know her. See, I was just a little upstart from out in the country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who is this woman?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: I don't think I know her. She must be a Jew herself. She has such a big nose. So I was still standing on my feet waiting for the answer. So I said to her, well, the word is Jewess, not Jew. I think she's a Jew herself. She has such a big nose. So I said, the word is Jewess. And I sat down. And, well, the other ladies came and apologized for her to me, but that was what this little fat farm wife did. At that very meeting. Of course, I was just a guest there. I should have kept my mouth shut, but I couldn't do it. So a year later, we were in the war and England was standing with our back to the wall. And the FBI came to Moscow to investigate this lady because she'd been a spy taking university students through Germany. And, of course, the first place they said to her was, right out, go out and talk to Lola Clyde. She'll tell you about the lady. So I told them about how she said, I think she's a Jew herself.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she teach at the university?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: She was connected with the university. I thought maybe she taught German or something. No, but she was connected with the university. But that is a well -remembered factor on Moscow. And she stood up to her. Oh, yes, because the rest of them, of course, were too polite. They were all, she was the guest speaker. But I was just a guest too, and I just wasn't going to stand there and listen to her go on that way. So that was the response anyway. But I was never much inhibited, you know, by any of it. The University of Idaho, you know, before they had a university, he said, I had the IDL presidency. He said, I had no faculty, no buildings, and best of all, no alums. And I was sent by the head of the history department of a big bunch of books to return to Judge Forney. And he lived in that house. And it's not the first house built in Moscow, like that miserable book says. It was built much after a lot of the others. But anyway, I knocked at the door, and Mrs. Forney came to the window, and she thought I was a fuller birch man. She started motioning me for me to get away. I had a briefcase with me. She thought I was an Avon peddler, I guess. Avon wouldn't have done her any good. But anyway, she motioned me to get out of there. So I just hollered in at her. I said, Mrs. Forney, these are the judge's books. And if you don't want them, I'm going to throw them here on the porch for you. And I did. I just opened the briefcase and threw them on her porch. So Mrs. Forney got her books back, and then she opened the door to see what it was about. But I was gone by that time. That's how long ago I was working in the history department. That sounds like an elite attitude to you. Yes, and she thought I was a peddler, and she was motioning me. So I just dumped the books on her porch. And then we had Mrs. Hodgins, who had been an agnéu from Boise, and she was quite one of the elite. And Premille French, the Dina women, cut quite a swath, and she set quite a pace at her. Do tell me a little bit about her since she mentioned her. I've heard some of the old male alumnis of the college talk about Mrs. French. They certainly remember her. She had very strict ideas. If the young men called on the girls three times, she ushered them into her little private room and asked what their intentions were. And most of them, even in those days back in the gay -roaring twenties, said, I just got the worst intentions in the world. And I don't know what you're going to do about it, Premille. But she was quite a gal.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would they dare call her Premille?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Oh, I think some of them were laughing a little about it. My intentions are very bad. I've got devious desires. So, anyway, one lady that entertained, they had lavish, very lavish parties and lots of cocktails. But she gave the word to these little serving girls, be sure and put three glasses of orange juice on the cocktail tray. Because one of them is for Dean Premille French. Be sure there is some orange juice for her. But the little serving girls always noticed that the three orange juice glasses came back untouched. So Premille must have had her a few of those little cocktails. But the little girls weren't quite that stupid. They put them on anyway since they were requested to do it. But anyway, she was very charming. She was a large, bosony woman. A real Dean of Women type. And she sailed majestically through the halls and laid down these rules and regulations for the youth of the land.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How understanding do you think she would have been of young people's problems?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: I'm talking about now as the Dean of Students today, the college is expected to take care of all kinds of psychological problems if he's good or she's good. All I can say is Dean Premille, how I wish you were back here now. How I wish you were on the campus now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you mean that just because of the moral tenor on campus?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: She can educate, yes I think so. It might turn her hair white prematurely. Yes, that's right. I think it would be alright. But I mean for kids' problems.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If they had a problem, do you think they could come and confide in her?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: No, I should say not. She'd sail majestically in, you know. I think most of them thought she was quite a hypocrite, and I think so too. Not that I ever ran afoul of her because I was an older girl. I'd been out teaching school and I was one of the older girls and studious and I knew what I was up there for. But I do think she was very kind or understanding or helpful with the problems if the girls got pregnant. There was just one thing, get out of university. Yeah, that's right. And she called after quite an affair up at the university and one of the professors was involved. She called in all the girls and gave them a lecture. She said, now you must remember Dr. So -and -so isn't quite himself. And one of the girls spoke up and said, that's right Dean, with all the talent there was going to waste around here, he never would have picked on that girl if he had been himself. And she was shocked. She was shocked that little gal nearly got kicked out of school over such a break as that. For saying that. Yeah, so she wasn't very understanding. But she was a good politician.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this a case of a girl who got pregnant?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, yes. One of the university faculty members. So this other little gal said, well if he had been himself he never would have picked on that one with so much talent around. So they've had these problems going on for a long time. Oh, the flapper era. Yes, that's right, the 20s. The roaring 20s. I've had the idea that mores were really changing at that time. That's right, they were. Yes, it was the era of the hip flask, you know. And you go to a dance and all the boys had a hip flask. That was part, that was regular. It was the regular adornment. And the gals had the big strings of beads, you know. I had been out teaching school and I was older when I went back to college and I graded papers in the history department. Tons of them, hundreds of blue books. This was after your kids were... No, this was when I was in college myself. This was before I was married.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Before then?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes. You were at the university again. Yes, I was at the university.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What years were these?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: This was 26 and 27 when I was there. And like I say, I graded history, oh, 400 blue books maybe a week for history. And it took 22 hours. One semester I took 24 hours and they took two credits away from me. And I had all A's. All A's the second semester and I had 24 hours.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: From what you saw about what the kids were doing on campus, was there lots of social life?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did the museum come?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Oh, fine, fine. All of it's falling apart. Here, come and take a look at this, Daddy. Come and see. These are real minds. Well, yeah, there was lots of social life. There was lots of this queen business going on. I had no time for it. You had 22 and 24 hours. Your nose is right at the grindstone, you know. And I graded for English. I graded the English department for George and Willie Miller. And I was on the debate team. I was on the, I did dramatics. I did two plays each semester.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you live at that time? Were you in a sorority?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: No, I lived at my mother's house and came in from the country every day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How far out was that from you?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: It's about four and a half miles out and I walked most of the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really, you walked in and out?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yeah, I walked a lot of the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Daily?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, not daily. Sometimes I did have an old car that we drove part of the time and I caught a ride with some neighbors that went to the convent sometimes. But lots and lots of times I walked because I'd be too late to catch a ride going home. I'd have to stay for play practice. I'd have to stay for, to go on debate teams, places, and for practice.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this one year that you did this?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: I did it. I did it one year. I'd only gone one year. I mean, one year it was still normal. One year to the University of Idaho and the rest I did by correspondence in summer school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, let me, there's a, guys, there's a couple of things about the, you know, I've heard a lot of, I've heard men talk about the fraternities at the time. And it seems like a lot of the countrymen did not, boys, didn't go into fraternities. They had too much money. Yeah, too much money. And they have mixed feelings about them, about the fraternities system. It was rather exclusive. Oh, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were the sororities the same way?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Oh, yes. In those days they were very exclusive and they were very snooty and, yes, that's right. There was lots of heartache and heartbreak over them. There wasn't on my account, because like I say, I was 26 years old. I'd been a school teacher and I was ambitious. I wanted to make good grades. And with all the hours I took and all the papers I had to grade, there was no room left for anything else.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were there for business strictly for business?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yeah, sometimes the girls, yeah, I was mature. And sometimes the girls at Foye Hall would invite me to come and stay all night with them if it got too late. And they knew I had so far to go home. They would invite me to come and stay and I did. And some of those girls are still my lifelong friends. Those girls would have me come there and stay and I did that. I had no time for it. I went into a couple of honoraries, an education honorary, debate honorary, and so on. And the thing I had to ask was, if I had to memorize a lot of stuff, I don't want in because I haven't the time to go memorize it. And they took me without memorizing all the rigmarole of stuff.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But do you know cases where girls' hearts were broken by not doing a sorority?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Oh, yes, girls. And the old mothers, the silly mothers back home and pounded into their heads. They had to make sororities and one girl tried to kill herself that very year because she hadn't made a sorority. And it was just ridiculous, you know. It was ridiculous. And some of them even, they would divide them up according to what church had you gone to. Well, if you'd gone to a certain church, then you would join this sorority. Gee, you or the church members would just simply be so mad at you. And there was just too much emphasis on it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did that mean that the sorority and fraternity life could easily just make it more difficult for the kids to study? I mean, just take them away from the studies?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, I think so. You'd see the big long list of chapter names they had to memorize and the national officers and then all the pledge of allegiance and symbolism. Get all that stuff memorized, you know, in order to be initiated. I don't know how they ever got their lessons. But that time in there, getting their history, they'd have been better off. You know, I just thought it was a waste of time myself. But when my girls got that age, they all joined and I let them. I said, well, you just go and find out for yourself, you know. That's what you want. But they had it a little easier because they had more money and things to go on. And they were all real good students and most of them could use good students. And that was one good thing about the sororities. They made them keep their grades up and that was good. Because they put them down on the study table and the older sisters helped the younger ones and all that was good in that respect. And it did, I think it had a good effect on their morals because if they hadn't been pretty fine little gals, none of the sororities wouldn't accept them. That's another thing I want to ask you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In those days, in the 20s, do you think that relations before marriage were really very uncommon?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Oh, sure they did. I think there were lots of babies born, lots of girls had to quit school in a hurry and go home. It happened all along. And there were mothers covering up, you know. They just weren't so open about it. Because then it was such a disgrace and such a stigma and so on and so forth. And poor little girls would be about to kill themselves over it. And I think that there was just as much going on, only it was all kept so in the dark. And it was so much more disgraceful and terrible, you know. Nowadays the gals have sense enough to say, oh they did it on purpose. They're going to raise their babies.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But like being French, do you think she probably knew that it was going on?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Oh yes, she was sticking her head in the sand. She wanted to make believe it didn't. And none of her little girls would think about it. And oh, she must have known. She had a lick of sense and all that. But it must have been a lot more difficult for kids in those days compared to now. When it's sanctioned you don't have to worry. No, no, they don't have to worry. I think it's better. I think it's better for their mental health. Better for their physical health. It's better all the way around. I think they've come a long way myself.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was birth control information available in those days in the 20s? Could kids get it in college?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: No, no, no, no, no, no, no, no, no, no, heavens, heavens. I had four babies in four years and I was one of the, I was one of the smart girls. And you know what I mean. And oh, there was great hush -hush and doctors wouldn't even tell you. No, after I had four babies. Why, they wouldn't even tell me then. No, no, the great hush -hush and secrets. I didn't realize it was so bad but it doesn't surprise me. Oh, just the way it was. No, you didn't talk about such things even. Uh -uh. You didn't talk about such things. I had a boy from India here and he was a foreign exchange student. And so he told me, he said, I'm going back to India and I'm going to make two blades of rice grow for only one grew before. I'm going to cut India's birth population in half. I'm going to cut it in half. He said, now, mom, I want you to tell me all about Planned Parenthood. And I said, I'll tell you, honey, the way it is. You start in planning after you know there's going to be another baby. He said, babies don't know a darn thing about this Planned Parenthood. The best thing you can do is go up there on the campus and ask those fraternity boys. I think they can clue you in, honey. So I saw him again just a year ago. He said, I've done pretty well. I have 11 sisters and they're all doctors. And in India the women of doctors serve the women. And after they've had two children, my sisters tell them that's enough and gives them the means, gives them the pill. And I said, well, I think that's just fine. Now you're just really working. And he says, I sell shell fertilizer. I fly my own plane. And he was from Madras and I am making three blades of rice grow for one group. I'm doing, we're doing all right. So you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So do you think that the mores in college and the university were different than they were in the country, say, in the rural areas around?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, I think so. Yes, I think the girls out in the country, they were more intent on just getting a man and grabbing some men and marry them and they're all settled right there. They weren't going on to college. They weren't going out for careers. They wanted to become a farmer's wife, most of them, and most of them just did that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Does that mean that they were much less likely to be fooling around, say, before marriage in the country?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: No, I think it went on to every place because most of the gals seemed to think that was one way of catching a nice young farmer was just to get pregnant and by golly, we'd just have a shotgun wedding and there we'd be and they had her made. And they were common, I know. Oh yes, I think it was just as common, you know, and I think it was just as common then as it is now. I really do. I don't think they need to decry the young people. I mean, they just know better now. They are freer, they are more open, they are smarter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you think there was a difference in attitude now?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: I'm thinking now about the people who were of high status in Moscow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a difference between those who had high status like the McConnells who were naturally, who had definitely achieved high status, and those who were kind of the social climbers, who were upwardly mobile, you know, as they say now? Do you think there was a difference in their attitude?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: I mean, I have been told by some people that the people who were genuinely of high status didn't try to show it, they weren't ostentatious, and that other people who were up and coming were always trying to put the other people down. Yes, I think that's true in any society. We had real ladies and gentlemen in town, just real ladies, fine people, fine good Christian women, and Clara Sampson's mother was one of them, Mrs. Moody, just one of the sweetest, kindest Christian women ever lived, and they were always helpful and kind and doing the right thing because it was right, and trying to be good Christians and good neighbors, and they weren't too concerned about the... all the little fashionable tricks and traits. There were a lot of real good, fine, bright, brilliant women, and with the coming of the university they really brought in a new dimension. Lots of those fine early -day professors' wives were smart and kind and gracious and highly intelligent, well -read, and they brought a new dimension into the community. Were they very involved in the community or were they more... Well, for a while there was a great division about this cap and cap, town and gown stuff, you know, the women, the town women would have a society of one kind, and then the faculty women, and then one year we were going to have a faculty wife for president, and next year the town women get to have it. You know, they went on exaggerating the difference between town and gown, but when I was a young farm wife and my children were growing up, I found that I had far more in common with the faculty wives than I did with the town wives.
