We were more interested in the same thing. We were interested in bettering the conditions for everybody's children. That's why I worked hard on the health unit. We had no health unit here, and when I had taught in the normal school in South Idaho, my, we just routinely vaccinated every child, gave them all their shops right at the school, and when my children started school here they had none of that. So I started questioning too, and many of the faculty wives had come from places for sure. It was just routine. They went to the schools on vaccinating. We even had dentists come out and fill the children's teeth right there at the school for 50 cents a child, and if anybody couldn't pay for it, the school paid for it. And we bought all the books in the normal school for I taught, and there were a lot of things that were, and I became, I started the PTA here in Moscow, and was the first president because I thought, my goodness, that's one thing where I can use my education and my training and the things I've seen done out in other places.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When was that about the PTA started?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, it had a little mother's club before that. I think it must have been about 35 probably. And about that time we put in the health unit. Ed Snow, my next door neighbor down here, was county commissioner. And so the first thing he did was put me on for the health unit to head a committee. But what you're saying then is, it seems to me that the faculty women tended to be more interested in those civic... Right. They were more interested. And they were the people I could count on as head of PTA. I'd get the faculty women, they would vote 100 % for these things. And the town women took less interest. A lot of them, of course, were picking peas and going all day and couldn't come to PTA. But even the middle class tended to be... Even the middle class, they tended to, well, my husband, I can't take a stand on that, it'd hurt my husband's business, you know. But the faculty wives were the ones that really saw the need for these things. So there was a certain amount of controversy over having a health unit. Oh yes, of course there was. Yes, there was a controversy. Is that because they were... Well, it was socialized medicine and we were sticking out our necks and yet we just were getting over the depression, just coming out from under when nobody had had doctors. So we were all just struggling along. But this was something we had done back in the school and it was a wonderful thing. And I worked hard on that, getting the PTA going, because there were so many things we could do. And we ran an exchange there at the school. We'd bring in all the outgrown overshoes, for instance, and put them in big boxes and the upcoming... Some other mother could take those overshoes our kids had outgrown. We didn't sell them, we just gave them. We had a room set up. We had a room there of patterns for little dresses for girls. We got through with patterns in there, it got too small, we took them in there. Next mother could check them out. And remnants of clothing. We'd bring them in. We had great button boxes. The library now has a button box where people go and get buttons. Well, we had it at PTA. They'd go in and pick out buttons. Warm winter coats and caps. Instead of getting them to a rummage sale, we hung them in the school. And PTA had women volunteers. And you go in and see if some of these coats and caps would fit your kids. And it made it more democratic because everybody went. We all went. And it wasn't that we'd gone to a rummage sale and bought them for next to nothing. We went in there and we took somebody else's clothes for our kids and gave ours to somebody else smaller. And those were all good progressive things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Good progressive things. I put in the hot lunch program too. The government was just starting to sponsor it. And there was a big argument with the school board that heavens wasn't that something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What more would the district have to do?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: And so I served on that committee to put in the first hot lunches and help cook the first lunches.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think that was?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: That was about 35 and 36. Along in there. Maybe 34.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there some people who didn't see the use of PTA at the time?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Oh yes, we were a bunch of upstarts. They didn't want any PTAs around there either. Yes, that was no good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why would they not want the PTA?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, next thing we'd be wanting more new schools. And that was right. We carried the ball for more new schools. Of course we did. We wanted a new high school and we got it. And all those were good things. And all of that came about through PTA work. And it was good work. And we had fun at the same time. We did a lot of good things. And the thing that made it so important was it was the teeth of the depression. People were still. And we put in the lunch and milk. Everybody had to have milk. Give them the half pint of milk right there. And if anybody couldn't afford it, why let the government subsidize it. And of course, oh my, that was just terrible. But we did it in spite of them anyway. And now they wouldn't get along without the hot lunch. But there was lots of objection. Yes, I remember the superintendent of the school saying to me, well Lola, the first thing you'll be wanting is to subsidize mothers to have babies. And so I said to that superintendent, I said I could just use a little subsidy myself. I have four bright ones for you. And I feel the government should subsidize me. I've got four of them for you. And they're all going to be good, smart children get along in school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And, but he said, well wasn't that nasty?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: He says to me, you'll be wanting to subsidize these parents for having children. I said I could use a little subsidy right now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So that's another example of really mostly women doing the work, isn't it?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, yes. There weren't so many women working then. The married women weren't teaching. And we did all that PTA business and that was good. And we would volunteer our time and go up there and help them pick out the coats and the overshoes that were outgrown. We kept track of it. We checked out the patterns.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Now the distinction between the university women and the love women we were talking about, that was still existing in the 30s. Yes, it was still there because even in PTA they wanted to have a faculty woman, they wanted to write this into the constitution when we wrote it. One year we'll have a faculty wife and the next year a town wife and they did that a couple of times. Finally I said, well that's just ridiculous. What we are talking about is mothers. We're not talking about wives. We're talking about mothers of children.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: And what difference does it make which mother?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Where they are. A mother of a child is going to be president. So we got that done away with. And then of course after the war and the Red Cross work and everything came along, we quit a lot of those activities because then they became accepted. It was accepted they'd have hot lunches. It was accepted and then a little later after that they consolidated. We had consolidation at the rural schools and it was another ball of wax. Then I went on the school board and I helped put through the books to the children. We would furnish books and rent them to the children. We wouldn't give them to them for nothing. We would rent the school books to the children. And that was good. But you would say that you counted more on the faculty wives as being progressive elements. Yes, that's right. And really the people you could count on to help you and anything that was good and forward looking to the community you get the faculty wives to help you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think there was much active antipathy between the faculty wives and the town wives?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, in the early years. And even yet there is some feeling, you know, well we're not educated. These faculty women, I think they're still a little even. Sometimes when I tell them I belong to the League of Women Voters, oh my, some of the old time women think that's almost as bad as being a communist.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, and I told them that I belong to Common Cause. The other day someone was asking me about who I was going to vote for. I said, well I don't know because I haven't studied it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I belong to Common Cause and one of these dear little women at the church said, isn't that some kind of a communist front? Was the League going in the 30s?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: No, no, we didn't get it going until the 40s. We didn't get it going until maybe the 50s, maybe the first of the 50s. Let's see, the League was going about 49 when I was on the school board because I joined it then. We were wanting to get through a bond issue and those women got out there and just worked like dogs getting that bond issue through. It was League of Women Voters that put it through.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For the school?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: For the school, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the League a very controversial group here to start with?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much opposition to it?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: It certainly seems like uppity women, you know, getting together and making up their own minds about these things. Well, no, I always thought it was great stuff. Well, I certainly would agree with you on that. I thought it was great stuff. And a lot of the women though that had been brought up in this conservative republicanism, they took a look at League of Women Voters and they said, well, they're all democrats. But that isn't true, you know. Of course that's not true. And if they've allowed it to become that way, I haven't... I don't go anymore at my age. But if they allow it, it's their own fault they should have all been in there pitching equally. They expect them all to come. It's open to everybody. And if republican women sat back and didn't go, then...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: and democrats dominated it, well, whose fault was it?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: So, but I had heard that from republic... conservative republican women.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were the college women very active in the churches too? As active as the local women?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, lots of them, yes. Most dedicated, especially in the earlier times. And oh, heavens, they couldn't run the churches without the faculty women.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh really?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Oh heavens, they... our faculty women really carry on the brunt of the church work for us.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they in the earlier years too?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, they were always dedicated, devoted people from the faculty down there pitching. When I think of the Dean Eldredge's for example, they were the backbone of the presbyterian church, the Axtell's. Just backbones. Oh, and when you're at Lewiston you should talk to Mildred Axtell. Her husband is on the faculty at Lewiston Normal. I think he retired now. Hensley, Kenneth Hensley.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know them?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: No, I don't. Her name is Hensley. Yes, Mildred Axtell Hensley. And their name would be in the phone book.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were her parents?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, her father and mother were on the faculty at the University of Idaho. He was a foreign language teacher and they were great presbyterians, awfully hard working people, brilliant people. There were five daughters, all of them five beta kappas. Mildred's a five beta kappa. She is just a brilliant girl. And she'd have a little time now. Her husband was, well he was like vice president over all the whole thing down there. He was the old superintendent of all the buildings and grounds and he was vice president of everything. I want to ask you a little bit too about the revivals in those days. I've heard some interesting things about, for instance, the Bulgin and the Bulgin revival. I don't know if you remember Bulgin or not, but there were evidently quite a few. And what I was wondering about is, I've noticed this feeling of a real distinction between those people that seem to be in favor of the more emotional revival kind of evangelical type of religion and the others who feel that's emotional. Picturians went much for that. They were more like, well other splitter religions would have big revival meetings and even put up a tent, you know, and have a sawdust trail, sawdust floor, and a tent and big bleachers. They'd have kind of camp meetings even out of the foot of Paradise Hill, I can remember, when they had a big tent there for revival meetings. And my father didn't even encourage any of us children to go down to the revival meeting. He said, oh, they get carried away, some of them. But there were many people like Billy Sunday, you know, and he was more like a Billy Graham, I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he here in this area?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: I don't remember if he ever came here or not. I think he did, though. I think Billy Sunday was in Moscow at one time. But my father had very little empathy toward the revival, meaning he thought that religion and Christianity was something that lived in your heart forever and that you didn't need to be revived. It was there all the time, and you go on in your way and let your light so shine before men that you didn't need to revive it. And that was all right, too. But all the Presbyterians have generally been a lot like that. But many others have believed in the, you know, we had, oh, maybe Southern Baptists, maybe Shaukhine Baptists, you know, and Holy Rollers. And there were elements like that that would, maybe they didn't have a church even in Moscow, but they'd come in and have a big revival meeting, and there'd be a big fanfare and lots of good music and singing. And lots of people wanted to hear the good singing and the good music. And some people liked a little sensationalism and preaching, you know. But... Entertainments. Yeah, that's right. But the Presbyterians have always been rather conservative, and it was something that your light so shine before men that all men can see it, you know, and that's the better way. So... There was, I think I've heard people say that they didn't believe that it was a real authentic change, that it was something that would just last for a little while. Yes. I think a lot of times it was that way. You know, lots of times it was that way. They were carried away by the emotionalism of the crowd and everybody walking down, you know, to confess their sins. Some of them didn't have any sins to confess. They were pretty good old people, you know, and they didn't need to get so carried away. But it was one form of entertainment. We must remember this looking back. They had no television. They had no telephones. They had no radios. They were just absolutely devoid of things to entertain them. The little school programs where the kids got up was about all they could really count on, or a little debate society, or a little literary society among their own people, and that was the sum total of their entertainment. So anything going on... Would attract people. ...would attract people. Well, you know, we're talking about religion, and I really have to ask you about Frank B. Robinson and about Psyche Anna.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was your opinion of him and his work?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, I thought he was really a very kind -hearted, you know, nice person. The first person, any kind of cross, cross, any drive in town, everybody would ask Frank Robinson. He'd hid the list a hundred dollars. He was always ready to help. But I don't really think he believed in his own Psyche Anna business, because he used to laugh with some of the men and say, well, there's lots of fools in the world, or something to that effect. And he kept it rather quiet in town. He didn't like to sell the copies of his stuff on the local level. He sold it around the world, the lessons of it. But he didn't... He was very, very kind and very fine citizen, and I loved his beautiful wife, and I loved the kids. Yeah, they were fine kids, both of them. And I loved Mrs. Pearl Robinson. She was just a lovely, gracious, beautiful lady. And I never studied the religion much, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you really feel that he did not believe in what he was teaching, in his Psyche Anna teachings?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, I never really talked enough to him, but... I don't think all these flamboyant, advertisement about, I talked with God, literally, and actually I did. We all talked with God, with our gods, you know. And I think Frank, I'm sure Frank Robinson talked with his God, you know, because he did so many lovely, fine, open -hearted, generous things.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: But I... too many people kind of misinterpreted what he was saying, I think, too. I never made a study of the lessons. I never got them, I never... I did read his crucified gods galore, and I agreed with him. There have been... nearly every religion has its own form of Jesus Christ, who was sacrificed and died to save the world. Fight, fight. There were lots of them, and lots of them, almost the same name as Jesus Christ. And there was nothing wrong with that crucified gods galore, and Dr. Drury, who was our minister all that time, he thought Frank Robinson was an awfully smart, brilliant man, and he agreed with him about... there were many Christs who had been crucified, that history had a lot of... They discussed religion quite often. Oh yes, they talked, they did together lots, and were very good friends, and very friendly about it. It was very open, fine, intellectual discussions they had. I read his blood on the tail of a pig, and I thought that was inferior to his crucified gods galore. And the strange autobiography of Frank Robinson... Well, I just... I'm so... I enjoy the family so much, and I'm such an admirer of all the fine things he did, that I just am not a fit person to talk about his religion, because I never read it, I never studied it. I did read, like I say, his crucified gods galore, and I agreed with him, there were plenty of gods. The strange autobiography I've read, and it's an interesting... I found it a very interesting book. The whole matter of his prosecution, or his statements on his passport, which he said that George Lanphier brought against him, was a very bitter episode, it seems to me, and he suffered a great deal in that. And I'm just wondering what your impressions are, like reading that book. Does that...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: why was there this strong antagonism between the two of them?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, they each ran a newspaper. You see, George Lanphier ran the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it called Daily Star Mirror then?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes. And he ran the News Review. Mr. Robinson ran the News Review, and there was lots of rivalry. And George Lanphier was a Democrat, and his paper always upheld the Democratic side of everything, and the News Review was more Republican.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he a Democrat, Lanphier?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes. Oh yes, very strong. And of course it was Depression years, too, and there was lots of bitterness over Hoover, and all that sort of thing, and it was the New Deal, and everybody was out hooping it up, you know, for Franklin Roosevelt, and the new look on everything, the CCC camps, and the WPA work projects, and it was quite controversial between them, you know. But I know that during... Dr. Drury and I headed Red Cross, and I worked on the board with him all those years through the Depression, and as he said, there was no cloth to wrap a baby in. We'd take newspapers, newborn baby wrap them in a newspaper to protect them. No milk. They didn't want them to sell milk in gallon cans for 25 cents. It might be germs in it. Well, Dr. Drury said, Let them have the milk. The mothers can boil it if they are afraid of germs. I don't think this milk's going to hurt anybody. Be as clean as you can, put in a gallon can, let them have it. Let them have it for a quarter. And they did that sort of thing, and whenever Dr. Drury needed some extra money for the Red Cross, he went to Frank Robinson, and he was never turned down. We always got money when we needed it for things like that, and boy, those are Christian.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's Christianity in action, isn't it?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: And I told you the sweet story, the best story of all. We had a woman, they called her the Galloping Goose in Moscow. She was very retarded, you know, and very odd, and she walked on the road, everybody just hiked up and down. Whenever she'd see a lovely free meal someplace, she was hungry. She'd go in and eat. So one day she was at the Moscow Hotel, and the table was all set to entertain the state legislators, you know. They had lovely little red salads up all the places, you know. She saw that beautiful food, and she went in and sat right down. And of course the busboys and people came running in the heads up. They came running to ask your poor little old Ida right out of there, you know, so she wouldn't disgrace the meeting. And Frank Robinson rose in his majesty of six feet four, 225 pounds. He just waved the busboys away. He said, Ida is my guest today. She'll sit beside me and bring me her bill. And I love it. I just love it. That put everybody in the proper perspective. I will pay her bill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, people like that aren't all bad, are they?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: No. Well accepted by a lot of the businessmen on Main Street. He wasn't solid on Main Street. A lot of those people just didn't seem to like him. And I wonder if it was because of the polarization over this business with Lamphere, whether that is why he was not liked by a lot of people. I think so. I think part of it was that. Now one very nice thing that speaks so well of him is that when he had this trouble over entering the United States, one of the people who really defended him was Senator Bora. And Senator Bora wasn't given to just defending anybody. But he certainly went to bat for him and saw that Dr. Robinson was in. And he was really a very fine citizen and a very interested person. And like I say, I don't understand about his religion because I didn't read it. I never did read his lessons that he had. It was supposed to be lessons that you studied and I never read them. But the people that worked for him all said how good he was to them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You worked for him?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes. They did feel he was good to them. Yes. And the McGoffs lost a little boy out toward the golf course out there, out toward Robinson Lake in a big spring when the spring freshets were going off. And everybody, all the neighbors came and looked and they couldn't find him. And Dr. Robinson, at his own expense, hired a plane to come down and fly low over the wooded area out there and they found the little boy. And Dr. Robinson saw that the family were really hard up and he hired Mr. McGoffs to come on into town and help him down at the Psycheana Publishing House. And Mr. McGoffs worked there until he died. And they were really, you know, just really, he was awfully good to them. And as I say, all my relationships with him were just on that order. When we needed something for Red Cross, we would just run the first person we thought about was go to Dr. Robinson and see what he'd do for us.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about some of the religious denominations in town?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: He claimed that there were some that were just really disliked him. Yes, some of them were very bitter and they'd get up and pound the pulpits, you know, and that sort of thing. And even in our own church, Mrs. Robinson and the children all belonged there, were members. And like I say, our Dr. Drury was very personally friendly with him and agreed about the crucified God's glory. He said, he is so right about it. Many of the great religions of the world, they all have a crucified God who died for humanity. And he said, it's symbolic. And I know what he means. But anyway, when we built the new church, they wanted a spire. No, that wasn't the point. They wanted a donation from everybody. So they went up and asked Dr. Robinson. He said, yes, he'd give them $10 ,000. But he would put on a spire, the spire that pointed to heaven. And he would be dedicated to his lovely wife, lovely, gracious Pearl Robinson. And our minister and I, we both agreed. They'd be all right. But mind them, there was some of the old time, the old fundamentalists there. Well, one of them said, he'll want the sign, Psycheanna. He'd want to put up a big sign on top saying Psycheanna and have it flashing on and off all night. And they wouldn't take the money. And that was a lot of money in those days. Oh, gee, the whole church only cost $42 ,000. So $10 ,000 would have done a lot of good. And as he said, I'm not a member of your church, but I would do it honoring my wife and my children. And I thought there was nothing wrong with that. But he wasn't asking for this sign. It was just the other people interpreted it that way. And every time they looked at that spire, they'd have to say, well, Psycheanna Robinson gave it to us. So did I tell you that this Paul Harsh, the brother to J .C. Harsh and the meaning of the news, they are Christian scientists, of course. And J .C. Harsh's past editor, the Christian Science Monitor. And of course, that newspaper is like the Manchester Guardian. If you see it in the Christian Science Monitor, you know it's right. It and Manchester Guardian, we swear by them, you know. Well, he wants to come and write a story about Psycheanna Robinson. He's a great believer and a great admirer of him. He studied it. He can just quote me long. Oh, I have the loveliest letters here from Paul Harsh. And I must write. And I couldn't have him two years ago because my brother was dying, my other brother. But I've got to write and tell him if he wants to write up about Psycheanna, come on now. I saw him when he was here a couple of years ago. Yeah, I ran into him at the museum. If he comes back, let me know because I'd like to interview him. All right. Because he's the only true believer I've run into. He's a real believer. And Paul Harsh wrote to me just the other day. I'd lost his address. Now, just the other day I had another letter wanting to know if he could come now and do it. And I've got to write and tell him, come on and write about Psycheanna Robinson. And I did give him Dr. Drury's address. And he'd written to Dr. Drury. And Dr. Drury says, I have the only complete file of the lessons that Dr. Robinson sent out. And you'd be welcome to come and borrow them from the library because he gave them, I think, to Huntington Library. And he could get them there and use them. So, I've got to write to Paul and tell him to come now, that he's welcome to come and stay with me and write the book. Well,

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: was his wife really devoted to him?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, I think so. She seemed, in the book, that's how he presents her. Yes, I think she was very devoted to him. And he certainly lavished everything on her. And I remember the little girl was so sweet and so well liked. My daughters liked her so much. And there was a need in town for a youth center, some place where the kids could get together. And say those kids went down and talked to Dr. Robinson and he gave them one. He furnished it, he set it up, he got the machine phonographs to play, dance records so they could dance. Set the whole thing up. The first youth center in Moscow was Robinson's. You... I see...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yes, did Tommy drive up? Do you remember the vocal opposition to him?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, I remember that our church wouldn't take the $10 ,000 because... And they were the very conservative old time people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And some of the others, though, didn't they denounce him from the pulpit?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Oh yes, they'd be up pounding the pulpit. Yes, yes, lots of them. You know, lots of them. But I never went along with it. I thought that he was a really very kind, very fine man. And I always remember that standing up in the Moscow hotel and assuming responsibility for our poor little... For the poor little lady that they were going to put out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he have a stubborn streak?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, I think he did. Yes, I think he had strong likes and dislikes, you know, too. Do you think that those he was opposed to, he was really strongly... Yes, I think so. Yes, I think so. This woman you mentioned, who was...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you call her?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: The woman at the hotel at that time that was... Oh yes, we called her the Galloping Goose, because she walked all over the country. Her name was Ida, and she was rather retarded.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And was she around here for very long?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, she was quite a fixture here for maybe thirty years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did she manage to get by? Was she living with parents or something like that?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: No, she'd come all the way from Sweden or Norway, from back there. And she lived here, well she worked, she did day work mostly. She'd go out and clean people's houses and that sort of thing. But those depression days, I'm just sure she was hungry and she saw all these nice tables set so lavishly. And she was just going in, and she often went to church suppers and things where she didn't really belong, and would eat. But then they made her welcome at the churches. Maybe I should get going in time for you to get supper. Well, I got supper already. I got it in the... I got it in the... Yeah, my crock pot, I got it ready. I got supper ready. So I'm all set. But I am going to take one of these up now to this meeting tonight, you see. Because it's largely teachers that are coming, or wives of teachers. And I'm going to take one of those and I'm going to take one of Kenny Platt's books. And we'll give him a... Blacks couldn't stay at the Moscow Hotel. That was, you know, typical of attitudes in those days. Do you think that... What do you know about the... Well, I really don't know. I really don't know. There have always been some people, you know, and I imagine there are yet, you know, that would be prejudiced. I have never felt any prejudice in any way. I went to school with our one and only Negro family, with the children from that family. All of us kids were just crazy about them. They could run so fast and they were good to the little kids.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The settlers?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Uh -huh, the settlers. And we just thought they were wonderful. And we all, all of us girls just cried and cried when the settlers boys quit school because the big boys wouldn't be able to run us through and play black man with us anymore.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Black man?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yeah, we played black man and uh -huh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Oh, they'd run out to catch you, you know, and you'd run from one base to another. And then the man out in the middle, he was supposed to be a black man. But the boys, they'd laugh just as hard as anybody about playing black man and they'd be the black man too, you know. And then sometimes they'd take a hold of our hands and we'd run them through and they'd help the little ones get through and not get caught.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean the older boys liked to play with the other kids?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, uh -huh, and we all played together. And I remember us girls crying when the settlers boys quit school because they wouldn't be there to play black man with us anymore.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They quit to, they quit to move?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: They moved, yeah, they moved to town. They moved off the farm, they'd been farming. That's right. Moved into Moscow and boy, we just felt awful bad that the settlers boys had left us.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that they were fully accepted out there?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, I think so. I think out there in the farm, I remember many people, I remember one little, later on, Gene, who's my special friend too, you know. He was on the school board down in another school district and there were a couple of other gentlemen on the board. And this little girl that went out there to teach said, well I have three men on my school board and the only white man among them is Gene Settles. And he was very fair and very fine with everybody. And I never, of course my folks had no feeling of discrimination. Gene worked for us when I was a little girl and every evening he was planning for the state examinations to pass the eighth grade examination into, and prepare for high school. So my mother would send him down every evening and help him learn all the state and county officials and help him with his mathematics and help him with the things so he could pass. And he did, he passed. And oh, my mother was so happy because Gene had passed the examination, could go on to high school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would they go to the dances?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, as a little girl I never went to the dances so I really don't know. I really don't know. But they were certainly awfully well accepted out of that little rural school. I was in classes with the two younger boys, with Booker T., Washington Settles, and with John, who later became a teacher at Harvard, or Carver, at Carver College, and married a woman who was a professor there. They were both professors and came out here to Idaho to summer school a couple of summers. And she was a gracious lady too, in her own right. And I think Moscow has always just really been very, very friendly, especially the Settles family. They were just high class fine people. They weren't such hard things. I think there are some hard things about life. Good things are sorrowful bad things, and then we're not so sorry about leaving. If it were all the birds and the flowers on Paradise Hill, we'd want to stay forever. Yeah, that's right. When I was asking you about the size of families, the thing that I didn't finish asking you about was what happened in time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now when your generation was growing up and you were having children, didn't you tend to have smaller families than your parents had?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Oh yes, I think so. I definitely think so.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: I got some information from a fine old druggist down in Genesee. And when I was pregnant with the fourth child, he stopped me on the street. And he said, oh, I think you've had enough babies. And I said, well, I've been thinking so myself. And he said, you come in here and I will show you what to use. And he got me one of those little rubber contraceptive deals. And the jelly that went with it. And diaphragm. Diaphragm was what it was, and I used it. And there were four and that was it. And four was enough.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you think this was something that was not only you, but was common to many of the women who were your contemporaries?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Sure, many of them, yes. They didn't know what to do. It was such a secret and the doctors wouldn't tell you. And there was no Planned Parenthood. There was no nothing. But still that changed. I mean, they must have wanted to find out. We were all sure we needed to find out. And I even went to these health units that I'd put up that I'd helped organize and start. And, well, they were very reticent. They couldn't tell you. They were afraid. Yeah, they were afraid, I think. And my doctor, the doctor who delivered these children one right after the other, he said only to me, Oh, mother, you're just the kind of a girl who should have lots of babies. You, they come, you have them easily. You've had no trouble. And look at the fine, bright, husky children you have. You should have had a lot of them. That's what my doctor said to me. And so I said to the doctor, yes, we're always, and we always are able to pay our doctor bills, said I to him. Yeah, and that's something. But isn't that, I mean, just at the, in a way, isn't that a way that women, that men kept women in the home and I would take care of the kids and them. Hide their shoes and keep them pregnant. That was kind of a joke, you know, hide their shoes and keep them pregnant. Hide their shoes so they can't go and be in the world. That's right. And keep them pregnant. But do you think, now, you know, try and look at it historically.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think there was some sort of revolution that went on in your generation that the, that the women got, got wise and made the families smaller?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: I think so, yes. I think so. I know there was. I just know there was because, well, the Depression hit and there was no premium on big families then. It was, women were just frantic when they found they were pregnant again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And how are they going to feed them? How are they going to pay the doctor bill?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: You know, it was a frightening, terrifying experience. And I think that all of them just determined, now, now we've got, there's got to be a way out of this.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that it was generally the woman who decided not to have a big family rather than a husband in your joint decision?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, I think so. I think the women had to do it. I think so. I definitely think so. There wouldn't be something probably that the husband or wife would sit down over supper and just have a long discussion about it. No, I think not. I think not.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, then that was really as the women's feminist movement would say, they'd be taking control of your own body, right?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, that's right. Yes, I think that's right. I'm sure of that. Now, one more thing. The difference between a farm family, again, and these middle class town families we were talking about before.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: With these middle class ladies in Moscow, or in the teens and twenties, were they more likely to have smaller families than the other countries?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: They seemed to, yes. That's right. They seemed to have smaller families.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you think that was their own way for these women of trying to carve out more independence for themselves and to have more freedom in that kind of thing?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: I think so. I think maybe. And they had tended all along to have fewer children. And I don't know that it was that they were more intelligent than maybe they had access to more knowledge. They got out among them. They talked together. And over the years there were certain doctors who would perform abortions, you know. And there were people, and they were knowledgeable of where to go to find these doctors. But rural women didn't have access to that kind of information. And I think that that's right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, and this would probably be true of Moscow as compared to, I mean, a small town like Troy or Genesee or Kendrick would be more like the country, right?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, that's right. That's right. Yes. But in a city like Moscow they were more exposed to these newer currents. That's right, to these newer thoughts. I think that's true. I think that's very true. But the Depression wasn't all bad, you see, because out of it grew some forward -looking things. Like hot lunch program for the children, milk for every child, free inoculations, smaller families, Planned Parenthood. All those things grew out of the great necessity and the dire need of those Depression years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you feel that the Depression directly made a lot of them decide not to have big families?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Oh yes, I think so. I definitely. They couldn't support the ones they had. And I think it just drove them to see the great necessity of doing these things. I'm sure that's right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think there was much of a realization, a growing realization, that women as people ought to have more of a life of their own independent of just families?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, I think so. I think with the reading and the cultural revolution. I think there were more girls who went to college, for instance, and they came in contact with other thoughts. More girls right along were going to college all the time. And you studied sociology and you heard about limiting families. You heard about taking care of the ones you had, about the psychological care, and about self -fulfillment, and all that sort of thing. And I think those all had their effect. I'm sure they did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When we talked about affection in the families of these fellows, that actually happened?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Oh yes, that happened. You too, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would it be mostly in the country or in Moscow?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Oh, in Moscow. I think the Saturday night businesses, you know, and like at the dances, they'd get a few drinks and then they were looking for fights.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you actually remember a hearing of anyone tarred and feathered here?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, yes. I know of one case where they tarred and feathered and rode him on the rail, you know. And I knew him later in life too, and he said if he had to do it over again, he would have gone. He would have been glad to have gone. But his father, he was the only boy among a big bunch of girls, and my father just, and mother both cried so, and took on so that he asked for a farm deferment. Although he was able, you know, but then he...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that happen in Moscow or in Genesee?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, the folks lived about halfway between out, and the Moscow board was what gave him the deferment. But the man was sorry all his life for it. But he was German. Yes, he was of German descent. Well, my impression is that the German people were expected to be super patriotic, more so than almost anyone else. Some of them had to bend over backward. In fact, when they sold war bonds, they'd go out to these German farmers, you know, and just put them right on the spot. They'd made fortunes and made fine. And we expect you to buy so many thousand dollars worth of war bonds. And they did. Lots of them went all out, as you say, to prove their patriotism. They didn't want anything to do with this war. But a lot of them, of course, were... they'd have been brought up. This was their home country. This was just a temporary thing here. But their home and their loyalty were back in Germany. And there was some of that, oh right. But they were among the older people. They were actually...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: do you think many of them actually helped go back to Germany someday?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Maybe they had planned maybe to go back someday. But it was, you know, they had closer ties. They were older people. The old folks that had come, maybe half grown from Germany. And they always thought, well, make a fortune in America and go back to the homeland. And that was hard on them, I know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, this young man who was tarred and feathered, how could he have ever liked any of those people that had done that to him?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: I mean, if it was me, I think I would have really always held a guest. Yeah, resented it. Well, he didn't... The way people that he knew, I mean, he was. Oh yes, oh yes. The boys he... Grown up with. Grown up with, yes, yes. Well, of course, there'd been some drinking too, you know, and in the heat of it all, you know how those things go. So,

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: he didn't live to be an old, very old man, so it was all answered for him then, you know. It was all answered from, but I do know that he told me in later life that he was sorry that he had ever, that he hadn't gone. He said, I missed that wonderful experience. I wouldn't have done it myself, but my folks, he said my father and my mother both were so set.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think they beat him up too, as well as Tar and Feather?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, I... That doesn't really matter. I don't think it matters, I don't really remember, but anyway, they roughed him up an awful lot and it was bad, and they did put the Tar and Feather on and they rode him on the rail and lots of... That's the classic... Yeah, example, yeah. By the way, just one more thing that just reminds me of. The Ku Klux Klan. Yes, we had a Ku Klux Klan here in Moscow at one time, and they were out burning crosses even and going around in sheets and pillow slips, and it never lasted very long. And of course it was against the Catholics and not against the Negroes here. The Ku Klux Klan was an anti -Catholic group, and I think the people that belonged over people without very high -grade intelligence, rather, not very highly intelligent. And they did have a Ku Klux Klan going around here for a while.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This anti -Catholic feeling, would they say burn crosses at people's homes who were Catholic? Would that be the type of thing they would do?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, I think they were going up on Musco Mountain to burn them, as I remember. They'd go up on Musco Mountain so they could see it, and some of them wouldn't let them go because they set the trees afar. So they were out marching around in white sheets and pillow slips, crazy. What time... try to place this in years. I've heard it was early 20s.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Does that sound right?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: I'd say it's the early 20s, yes. I'd say it's the early 20s. 22.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did people react to them? Do you think that people were scared by them?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, I certainly wasn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I took a look at the kind of people that belonged, and I thought, well, just how stupid can we get?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: So it was known who belonged, more or less. Oh, it was more or less known, sure. And they had out these little anti -Catholic books, you know, and they were passing them around. And there was just a lot of hocus pocus, you know. Just ridiculous. And all you had to do was take a look at the kind of people that belonged to it, and you knew it wasn't very important.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were many of them from the South who belonged?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: No, I don't know. The ones I knew were... Well, you know, because the Klan did start in the South, I just wondered whether it was a Southern organization here. This wasn't against the colored people at all. This was just against the Catholics. But there were magazines published even in the Kingdom, some headquarters in the East, anti -Catholic things. Yes. And I remember once they had some kind of an escape nun. She was supposed to escape from a convent. She was going to tell all, and one of the churches sponsored her. And of all disgusting, revolting things, she'd get up and... I knew what you could tell the woman was mentally deranged. She just went on and on about the goings on. But, heavens, here are a lot of men standing around there guarding it. And someone said to me, oh, those are Catholics' grand members. They've come here to protect her so she can get up and talk. Heavens. Well, I certainly had no part in that and didn't want any part. I did happen to go with some women to hear this woman that night. I took some old lady who wanted to go hear all about it, you know. Sensational. Heavens, just disgusting. Charlie opened her mouth and I thought, well, she's a psychopath of some kind.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that the Catholics were relatively separate from the rest of the town in Moscow in those days?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Was there something that the Klan could try to... No, I don't think they could have driven a wedge in any way. Although I was brought up in this family where we had such great respect for religious tolerance. And my father had come from just out of Belfast, from Ramelton. Milford Ramelton, out of Belfast. And he had heard that all his life, the trouble. And he said it was just nothing but a source of trouble. We all worshiped the same God and some of our neighbors were Catholics. Some very good friends, first to come and help you, you know. And we were so very friendly with our good Catholic neighbors. So in Moscow you were. So in Moscow I was still just as friendly with the little girls that came down from the convent. I thought they had better manners, were better trained. And I never had any feeling at all myself. But I think maybe there were some. No, I think there were some that, oh, my dear Catholics. And even in high school, the kids that came over from the high school, from the convent high school to the Moscow public school, because it was broader and more kids there, some people had, some of the other girls in the school would say, well, if you're real stupid you have to go to the convent, but the smarter ones go to the Moscow high school. And that was so unkind. It had nothing to do with it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know why they didn't like Catholics then?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What the reasons they gave was not hope that they objected to us, something like that?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: I really don't know. They had no... Just one more controversial group that the Klan reminds me of, and that is not a group that had anything in common with them, because of the IWWs. And what I want to ask you about, now in the lumber country, the IWWs have a pretty good reputation among a great many people as having improved the conditions. But how about the... The view from Moscow was quite the opposite, as I understand. They really disliked them very much. Yes, they had no part with it. My husband's people here ran a threshing machine. The Snows, where I lived and taught school for four years, they ran threshing machines. And they had to be awfully careful who they hired. They had IWWs and they called them Wobblies. And they would throw a bomb into the machine, into the workings, and blow up the machines that summer. That was 22, I think, the summer of... Was that real as a danger, or was it something that... They did it a couple of times, yes. They did it here on the Clyde Farm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: IWWs did? Blew up a threshing machine?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes. They threw one in and it went off with an explosion. It didn't throw the whole thing to pieces, but it did explode in the machine. And they had... They were sure that it was IWWs that did it, and not just a smut fire. No, they said that this was IWW. One of the boys got sick here, and he was a card -carrying IWW. And the men took him to the hospital and so on, and took care of him. But he carried one of those black jacks, you know, on the end, the brass ball on the end, he carried that. He had an IWW card, and they were to notify the IWW. And there were a couple of others that were. And of course, they strove for shorter hours. And of course, the farmers were noted for getting up at daylight and working with a lantern from midnight.

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: And they fell in disrepute in these communities because of the violent way. Had it just been, we want shorter hours, and that sort of thing, better food. They had such wonderful food, all these women were super cooks, and they just each one outdid the other, trying to get the nicest meals in the world for them. So they couldn't complain about the food. I think that was more in the lumber camps they were complaining about. Yes, but they did complain about the long hours. And of course, harvest is a time when they have to work long hours to save the crop. So there were certainly two sides, but the farmers were very resentful of anybody that wasn't willing to go out and put everything into it for the few hours, for the short harvest season.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They didn't feel that the IWWs were shirkers when they worked, did they?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: No, they just wanted to quit too early, and they wanted to quit too early, and that began, they wanted shorter days, of course.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So really they found it difficult to hire without hiring IWWs?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, that's right. Because so many of the hands, the traveling people were. That's right, lots of the traveling people that came through were, and there was a feeling on the part of the farmers of resentment. But I think, once again, that was a good thing, because it got the farmers, it got new ways, it brought on combines and tractors, for you didn't need these great crews of men. You got one man who could farm half the country. They didn't need all this great army of men to work for them. But you feel sure from what the Clyde said, that they had no doubt that it was actually set by the IWWs. Yes, they thought that this one explosion was set by the IWWs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they know who did it, who they thought did it?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, yes, I think they thought that this man that later got sick and they had to take in, that was the card carrying man, they think that he was kind of a leader. They called him Blackie. Yes, they found his card and in his pocket. But I mean, in the machine, they found evidence. Yes, there was evidence that something had been thrown in. This was about 1922. Yeah, about 1922, I believe. This was after the First World War, and probably, about 1922, 1923.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The attitude towards these migratory workers that came through, what did the farmers really think?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, they were all, you know, the farmers were just hardworking people themselves. And in those days, the formula was for everybody to work hard, you'd make a lot of money, and you could have all these things. There was never any idea of asking for government subsidies and that sort of thing. You got out and you earned it yourself. If you wanted your children to be educated and to have things, there was nothing to do but you worked. You worked like that, see, and get up early and work late. And they couldn't see the other person inside of it. They were willing to do it, because it was their bread and butter on the line, their farms on the line. But they expected the men to do it, and that's where the trouble came. Yes, I cooked for the snowboys, and one noon, all the men lined up outside. After dinner, they ate a nice dinner first, and then they all lined up, and they weren't going to go back to the field, they were unstruck. And it took quite a lot of negotiating out there to get them back. And it was getting late in the harvest season, and there was just nothing to do but meet whatever demand it was. And it was met, and they negotiated and got it done.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know how much they negotiated?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Oh, it probably wasn't over fifty cents or a dollar a day. So it wasn't so much as all that, but anyway, it was all settled.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was one man their spokesman?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, they had one man that talked for them. I can't imagine it created goodwill between the two of them. No, it didn't. And what had been really fun and an adventure, when all the neighbors came in and did each other's crops, we'd do this one person here, old Mr. So -and -so, he needs the money, we'll go to him next. Now we'll do this. And then it had been a great adventure and fun. But when they started having to go out and hire outsiders, strangers that came into the country, that's what made it, took the fun and the joy out of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did they have to go to outsiders? What happened if they couldn't use just local people anymore?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, I imagine that over the years the kids had grown up and gone to that First World War. A lot of them didn't come back to the farms. There weren't big families. And then as the farmers got to farming, they had other ways of combining, I mean of threshing. The Clydes had their threshing right here. And those next door, they had their threshing machine. Each one had his own.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, they had their own threshing machine?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, they had their own threshing machines. And so many people with 500 or 600 acres, each one had his own threshing machine. And instead of all going together and having 22 header boxes and everybody doing this one, and then each one was doing his own thing. Then he had to get outside. Then they had to get outside.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they still steam threshers at that point?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, they were steam threshers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And they had the long belt between them?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, they had the belt. I wonder why they decided to get their own instead of... Because it seemed like it used to work pretty well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't it work when they all went together?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, it did work when they went together. It did. Well, I think it just was that they had such long... They would start early, like down toward the rim, and then come on up. But as each farm expanded and got more land, then they needed more threshing machines. Instead of this man and that man and that man each owning 160, there would be... One man with... One man with 500 acres, the next one with 500, the next one with 500. And if you had 500 acres, you almost had to go get your own threshing machine to get a cut. I think that was probably the way of it. But that's the way I remember the IWW deal. I just remember that one summer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That year of the strike, was that the same summer?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, it was the same summer. About 22 and 23. Those were the years. And these men, a lot of them had come from Colfax. Colfax is a little earlier harvest field than here, and they'd gotten none there and they'd come here. And these men had hired them. And they picked them all up in town. They had done trangents that had come in looking for harvest jobs and brought them out. And the men on the snow machine knew the men here on the cladding machine. They had known each other. Before.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they both strike?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Well, I don't think these folks struck. They just tried to blow up a machine that day. And then one of them got so sick. And in this process of identifying and trying to get word to his relatives and so on, they had to go through his pockets. And they found he was an IWW and that he had this blackjack and that he had come equipped for trouble.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did most of these fellows come from, these itinerant guys that were working the harvest? Did you know where they were from?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: I really don't know. I don't know where. I never did know where they came from. There were very few local people. There were two or three local men from town on the crew. But once again, they knew the Clydes, they knew the Snows, and they went right on with the work. And they tried to hold the thing together. Because they were personal friends and men who knew them. And men that were going to have to go on living in the community with them, too. But these others were trangents who had come in and were just waiting for harvest jobs to open. They put in time over at Colfax and followed on over here. It was a little later.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: After 1922, what happened? Did they have to still keep going to outsiders to thrash?

speaker: Lola Gamble Clyde
words: Yes, quite a while. There was quite a little of that around. But then they started going into combines. Starting about 1924 and 1925, everybody was buying combines because they didn't need so many men. Even back in 1922, Snows had had a horse -drawn combine. But then they got more horse -drawn combines. And then pretty soon there was tractor -drawn combines. And then pretty soon it was self -fulfilled. But all this trouble with the IWWs helped hurry up the getting of the combines into the field. It hurried. They figured it was the only way to relieve the labor problem was to get combines that they needed to run them in their own families. So I think that that's the way that worked. And then of course in 1929 came the Depression and that was a new ball of wax.
